title: Chapter 20: Locked Doors of Mercy
paragraphs: The ash stopped falling.

It was not a mercy of fading nature, but a physical preamble to catastrophe. Dawn never came. The air under the walls of Agdash suddenly became thick, viscous, and icy, as if blood had stopped in the veins of the universe itself. Sounds—the distant clang of elven patrols, the rustle of dry grass, the quiet breathing of the sleepers—all vanished at once, swallowed by a deafening, pressing vacuum.

Rene, sitting by the cooled campfire, was the first to realize what was happening. The thin stream of minty smoke from her cigarette stopped rising. It froze in the air in a motionless, curved line, bound by a suddenly changed gravity.
The fox slowly rose to her feet. In her yellow eyes was reflected a primal, cold horror.
"Wake up," she commanded quietly, but so sharply that it sounded louder than a scream. "Time is up."

Dzhanavar sprang up, instantly throwing off sleep. The fur on the werewolf's scruff stood on end; he bared his fangs, blindly sweeping his nose from side to side. Kirah let out a stifled moan, clutching his head with massive paws. The giant demon felt what was hidden from the eyes of others—the rotten, vibrating frequency of the Abyss, which echoed as pain in his own crimson veins.
Yashil raised his head to the sky.

The leaden clouds over the wastelands did not just part. They rotted alive. With a disgusting, wet sound of giant fabric tearing, space turned inside out. A colossal, pulsing black-crimson vortex tore open the heavens. It was a terminal necrosis of reality—a breach of such size that pure, undiluted darkness poured through it onto the dead earth.
The gate towers of Agdash exploded with the panicked ringing of silver gongs.

In an instant, the refugee camp huddled right against the city walls turned into a boiling cauldron of despair. Hundreds of exhausted creatures—gnomes without clans, half-orks, half-breeds, and transparent hydrols—rushed to the Great Gates. Their cries, full of animal horror, merged into a single, soul-vibrating wail.
"Let us in! We beg you!"
"There are children! Open the gates!"

Yashil rushed into the crowd. He saw a transparent hydrol mother, whose neon blue glow had turned almost gray from exhaustion, desperately pressing her child to her chest, trying to shield it from the encroaching shadow. An old gnome hammered with blood-scratched fists against the smooth jade of the gate valves.

On the balcony above the gates, the captain of the guard appeared—the very same one whose will Yashil had broken several days ago. The elf's face was whiter than marble. He looked down at the sea of hands stretching toward him, and his own hands, clutching the edge of the parapet, shook.
The captain's eyes met the eyes of Yashil standing below. In the elven gaze was a torturous, inner-tearing plea for forgiveness.
But the order of the High Elders from the White Hall was absolute. Elarion did not intend to waste the precious mana of the defensive dome on "filth" when the city was threatened by a global breach.
"Forgive me... spirits of the ancestors, forgive us all," the captain whispered with his lips alone. He abruptly turned away and waved his hand.

With a monumental, grinding groan of heavy gears, the small valves of the Great Gates slammed shut. Following this, the jade walls flared with a blinding white light—Agdash raised an absolute magical barrier. But this dome passed behind the crowd of refugees, cutting the city off from the outside world and leaving hundreds of lives outside, right to be torn apart by the Void.
The crowd at the gates froze. The cries died down. It was a silence of absolute, crystal-clear hopelessness. They understood that they had been betrayed.

From the bleeding hole in the heavens, Reapers began to fall. Multi-jointed, grotesque creatures with asymmetrical body geometry, covered in ashen-gray armor, landed heavily on the pavement stones. Their faceted eyes lit up with a dead, hungry light, picking out the defenseless crowd at the gates in the gloom.
Rene approached Yashil from behind and firmly grabbed his shoulder.

"We need to leave, Greenie," her voice was cold, devoid of its usual irony. The fox looked at the advancing avalanche of monsters. "We have a couple of minutes to hide in the network of old ravines. Agdash has locked its doors. These refugees are already corpses. We can do nothing. Let's go. Now."
Dzhanavar shifted heavily from paw to paw, gripping the axe shaft so hard that the wood squeaked pitifully.

"Wolves don't run from a fight, little fox," the werewolf growled, but in his eyes also was a calculation of the doomed. "But wolves don't throw themselves under a mountain collapse either. It smells like a slaughter here in which there will be no winners. Augh. Greenie, she's right. Time to scramble."
Kirah said nothing. The huge demon simply towered over them, trembling with horror before his former brethren, awaiting the order of the only one who treated him like a living being.
Yashil looked at the hydrol woman with the child. At the old gnome who had slumped to the ground, covering his face with his hands. At the hundreds of eyes in which the last spark had gone out.
The goblin softly but firmly shook Rene's hand from his shoulder.
"Run," Yashil said quietly. In his voice was neither condemnation nor false heroism. It was a calm statement of his own choice. "You are my friends. And I don't want you to die. Go to the ravines."
"And you?!" Rene almost broke into a scream. The fox's perfect mask cracked. "What are you planning to do, you idiot?! You have no magic! You have no sword! You weigh as much as a sack of feathers!"

"I am going to stand here," Yashil turned to face the advancing horde of Reapers. He stood exactly in the middle between the crowd of weeping refugees and the creatures from the Abyss. "The High Elves decided to turn away from them. If we also turn our backs on them... then Elarion was right. Then we truly are just filth surviving at the expense of others. I don't want to live in a world where that is considered the norm."

The little goblin dropped to one knee and picked up a heavy, rusty iron pipe from the ground—someone's dropped staff. He stood in a pathetic semblance of a combat stance; his thin green hands shook with tension, but his feet were firmly pressed into the ash.
It was the most ridiculous, absurd, and hopeless sight in the history of the fading world. One tiny, unarmed goblin against the army of the end of the world.
Dzhanavar looked long and heavily at Yashil's back. The werewolf closed his eyes, exhaled noisily through his nose, and with a swing drove his giant axe into the stone right next to the goblin.

"I hate my sentimentality," Dzhanavar grumbled, baring all his fangs in a frightening, deadly grin. His body began to swell with primal muscle mass. "You know, Greenie... a wolf, maybe, doesn't throw himself under a collapse. But he doesn't leave his pack under a collapse. Remember this day, bastards! Today we write quotes in blood! Augh!"

Rene let out a long, ragged sigh. She elegantly threw her half-smoked cigarette into the ash and crushed it with her heel. The fox unbuttoned the buttons of her heavy cloak, letting it fall to the ground so it wouldn't constrain her movements. In her thin, graceful hands, with a quiet click, appeared two curved, poisoned elven daggers.
"You have a disgusting influence on me, Yashil," Rene hissed coldly, standing on his left. "My makeup will be hopelessly ruined. But if one must die, then let it be in good company."
The earth shook.

Kirah stepped forward. The four-meter demon stepped over his friends, shielding them with his colossal, resinous body. His crimson veins flared not with the magic of the Abyss, but with the pure, undiluted rage of a protector.
"No one. Touches. My. Master," Kirah's voice thundered over the wasteland, drowning out the screech of the approaching Reapers.

There were four of them. A Goblin, a Demon, a Werewolf, and a Fox. Backs to the locked doors of the arrogant city, faces to the tearing reality. They were not heroes from ancient legends. They were outcasts.
The first wave of creatures with a disgusting hiss collapsed upon their pathetic line, and the silence of the fading world was replaced by a symphony of blood and steel.
